stem: Consider the system of linear equations \( [[a1]]x + ([[b1]])y = [[c1]] \) and \( ([[a2]])x + [[b2]]y = [[c2]] \). What is the value of \( x \)?
solution: Eliminating \( y \) (or applying Cramer's rule) yields \( x = [[answer]] \); the accompanying value is \( y = [[q]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the value of \( y \), not the requested \( x \).
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign, a common Cramer's-rule denominator error.
This adds the two unknowns instead of isolating \( x \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: Solving the three equations simultaneously (e.g. by Gaussian elimination) gives \( x = [[answer]] \) (with \( y=[[q]] \), \( z=[[t]] \)).
distractor_rationales: This is the value of \( y \), not \( x \).
This is the value of \( z \), not \( x \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A theater sold [[totalTickets]] tickets for a total of \$[[totalRevenue]]. Adult tickets cost \$[[priceA]] each and child tickets cost \$[[priceC]] each. How many adult tickets were sold?
solution: Let \( x \) be the number of adult tickets and \( y \) the number of child tickets: \( x+y=[[totalTickets]] \) and \( [[priceA]]x + [[priceC]]y = [[totalRevenue]] \). Solving gives \( x = [[answer]] \) adult tickets (and \( y = [[numC]] \) child tickets).
distractor_rationales: This is the number of CHILD tickets, not adult tickets.
This is off by one from a value derived from the wrong total.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Line \( \ell_1 \) has equation \( y = [[m]]x + [[k]] \), and line \( \ell_2 \) has equation \( [[a]]x + ([[b]])y = [[c]] \). What is the \( x \)-coordinate of the point where \( \ell_1 \) and \( \ell_2 \) intersect?
solution: Substituting \( y=[[m]]x+[[k]] \) into \( [[a]]x+([[b]])y=[[c]] \) and solving for \( x \) gives the intersection's \( x \)-coordinate, \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the slope of \( \ell_1 \), not the intersection's \( x \)-coordinate.
This is the \( y \)-intercept of \( \ell_1 \), not the intersection's \( x \)-coordinate.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Given that \( [[a1]]x + ([[b1]])y = [[c1]] \) and \( ([[a2]])x + [[b2]]y = [[c2]] \), what is the value of \( x + y \)?
solution: Solving the system gives \( x=[[p]] \) and \( y=[[q]] \), so \( x+y = [[answer]] \). (Sometimes adding a multiple of the two equations gives \( x+y \) directly, without solving for \( x \) and \( y \) separately.)
distractor_rationales: This is \( x \) alone, not the sum \( x+y \).
This is \( y \) alone, not the sum \( x+y \).
This is the DIFFERENCE \( x-y \), not the sum \( x+y \).
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by two from the correct sum.
This is off by two from the correct sum.
